schools.summary: The miracles confirm the doctrine; the Synoptics differ in order; the act and teaching fixed together in men's minds.
The descent = condescension to the sin-burdened who cannot climb the mount; the leper figures sinful humanity.
He shows in works the authority shown in teaching, that none think it ostentation.
A striking display of divine power, cleansing leprosy by word and touch.
Leprosy loathsome, contagious, humanly incurable, cured only by God; the leper's doubt is of Christ's willingness, not power.
The leper figures the sinner falling before Christ, confessing his disease and seeking penitence's cure (Remigius).
proskynein = the East's gesture of reverence, confessing Christ's power, not necessarily divine worship.
He left all to Christ ('if thou wilt'), making him Lord with power over all.
Touching a leper made one ceremonially unclean (Lev 13:3), yet the touch expressed and pledged the cure; sovereign 'I will'; instant cleansing.
He touched to show he was not subject to the Law and that to the pure nothing is impure; unlike Elisha he heals as Lord.
His purity repels all filth; taking our flesh he took our impurities yet contracted no stain, sprinkling us with his holiness.
Silence to avoid premature Messianic acclaim and thronging (Mk 1:45; Isa 42:1; Jn 6:15); the priest's sanction the appointed test (Lev 14).
Christ defers to the priests in humility; not destroying but fulfilling the Law; the typical sacrifices not yet abolished (Bede).
The cleansed leper, spreading the report, is to be condemned for disobeying Christ, the time not yet come (Jn 7:6).
A Gentile Roman officer at Capernaum; Matthew says he 'came,' Luke that he 'sent' elders/friends — one event, the deed ascribed to him as its author.
The centurion figures the firstfruits of the Gentiles, perfect in virtue (hundred = perfect); the move to Capernaum signifies turning to the Gentiles.
No two miracles but one; absurd to divide the harmonious accounts.

debates.question: Did the centurion come himself or send (Matthew vs Luke)?

sides.position: one event; Matthew ascribes to him what was done in his name

schools.summary: pais = servant/boy, fixed as 'slave' by Luke; a violent paralysis 'grievously tormented' (rheumatic fever/tetanus); 'lieth' = laid forth, prostrate.
He recounts the servant's state with grief to move the Lord; all ought to take thought for their servants (Rabanus).
'I am coming, and will heal him' — healed before arriving (v.13); therapeusō (serve/cure) vs the centurion's iathēsetai.
A short, full answer: an absolute promise offering more than asked — his presence unsought yet offered.
Christ here goes before the wish, offering to come, to reveal the centurion's worthiness.
Great humility ('not worthy') and a faith risen above magic touch to a delegated power resting only on Christ's will ('speak the word only').
Two points: sparing Christ as unworthy of a visit, and ascribing power to heal by a mere word — astonishing humility in a Gentile.
He saw the Divinity hidden beneath the body (Jerome); the Church echoes 'Lord, I am not worthy' at the Table (Pseudo-Origen).
Reasoning from his own command of soldiers: if men obey an inferior officer, how much more do diseases obey Christ's word; instant obedience.
Less-to-greater, not equality: a higher conception of divine power than of his own authority; Christ as Lord of all commands in absence.
The soldiers spiritualized as the angelic powers at Christ's command, who say to the disease 'Go.'
Christ's wonder is a teacher's example, not surprise in God; he created the very faith he marvels at; faith, not riches, is what God honours.
A Gentile's faith in the bare word, greater than Israel's craving for touch; perhaps the first Gentile convert; a proselyte of the gate (Lk 7:5).
Wonder belongs to Christ as clothed with human affections; Mary's faith greater absolutely, the centurion's praised in a particular respect.

outliers.note: Stray catechetical material on eternal life, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: 'East and west' = the whole world (Isa 45:6); 'sit down' = recline at the heavenly feast; meant to soften Jewish prejudice; Luke gives it elsewhere (13:28).
The master figures the future calling of the Gentiles throughout the world — strange to Jews who confined God to Abraham's family.
'Many, not all'; Abraham in the kingdom because the God of Abraham is the Father of Christ.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the mote and beam (Mt 7:4).

schools.summary: 'Sons of the kingdom' a Hebraism for the Jews, natural heirs by descent who forfeit it; 'the outer darkness' set against the lamp-lit night feast; the article makes it emphatic.
Called children because they held a place in the Church; the covenant gave them peculiar right until their rejection (Rom 11:16).
A historical prediction of the Jews' dispersion; weeping (heat) and gnashing (cold) of the scattered.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the gospel preached to all nations, unrelated to the verse.

themes: children of the kingdom cast out

schools.summary: 'According to thy faith' a general measure of God's dealings; the complete cure 'in the selfsame hour' fulfils v.7; one event with Luke 7.
Grace is poured out when the vessel of faith is open; God is limited toward us only by our unbelief.
Peter's wife's mother proves he was married (1 Cor 9:5), urged against enforced celibacy; bedridden with a sudden severe fever; on the Sabbath after synagogue.
Allegory: Peter's house = the Law/circumcision, the mother-in-law = the synagogue or weak Law, feverish with hate/infidelity/lust, healed by Christ's touch.
Peter's reverence: he waited rather than force Christ thither; Christ went in of himself to honour his disciple.
Healing by touch (cf. word, both); 'began to minister' (conative imperfect); the strong reading 'ministered to him'; serving Christ the proof of restored health.
Not enough that she is cured: strength is given besides, so she rose and ministered at once (no fever's usual weakness).
Evening = the Sabbath's close (and the cool), when they felt free to carry the sick; demoniacs abounded then; healed all 'with a word'; no miracle for display.
The Evangelist sums an innumerable flood of cures in one word; Isaiah brought in to forestall unbelief.
He sent away demons and diseases with a word, to show he came for man's salvation; the sunset shadows the passion (Rabanus).
From Isa 53:4; Isaiah states the atonement, yet Matthew (with the evening in mind) applies it to bodily healing; a free rendering of the Hebrew.
Matthew states not merely the benefit but the purpose: the bodily cures were a pledge of the deeper, spiritual grace of Christ's death.
A double fulfilment: highest by bearing our sins on the tree, lower by sympathizing and healing sin's fruits — an elegance of Scripture.

debates.question: Does Isaiah 53:4 here mean physical healing or the atonement?

sides.position: applied to bodily healing as a pledge/sign of the redemptive purpose
a double sense: chiefly the atonement, lower the healing of sin's fruits

outliers.note: Stray material on Paul's miracles, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: From Capernaum to the eastern shore (Gergesenes, v.28); a withdrawal for rest; 'the other side' = the lake's curvature below Capernaum.
He bids the disciples cross to teach lowliness and shun ostentation, not dismissing the crowds who might hope to follow.
His benefits not confined to one place; the necessities of souls call him to cross over (Acts 16:9).
An over-eager scribe, likely hoping for worldly advantage; self-confident, yet Christ deals gently; striking that a scribe is moved to discipleship.
Two would-be disciples with opposite faults; the scribe's ready offer sprang from imagining an easy life.
Christ's homelessness dashes the scribe's hope; to follow is poverty and privation; first use of 'Son of man' (Dan 7:13; Ps 8), a title of humiliation.
A warning not to expect rich hire from a master who himself lodges in borrowed houses; describes Christ's condition and the disciple's expected lot.
An already-disciple, over-cautious; uncertain the father was dead (may mean 'after he dies'); Tobit parallel; tradition (Clement) names Philip.
Opposite fault to the scribe: he does not refuse the call but asks delay for a filial duty, the father probably aged.
Two senses of 'dead': let the spiritually dead bury the naturally dead; to preach Christ is better than to bury (Chrysostom via Robertson).
Burial is not condemned (a divine command strengthening resurrection hope); only what withdraws us from the right course is called death.

themes: let the dead bury their dead

schools.summary: 'The ship' (Peter's, ready); Mark's 'as he was' shows exhaustion and sleep; Matthew keeps the order; other boats made the miracle striking.
Allegory: sea = the world's turmoil, boat = the cross or the Church, in which Christ crosses and stills the waves of persecution.
He takes only the disciples and lets them be tossed, to teach lowliness and prepare the 'victors' for strife.
A sudden whirlwind off Hermon on the low-lying lake; the ship filling; he slept on the pillow with a pure conscience, safe in God.
The storm arose by divine providence to try the disciples' faith (Gill); or stirred by Satan to drown the gospel's preachers (Clarke).
A type in Jonah, unconcerned and asleep amid danger; all creation is conscious of its Creator.
'Save us at once, we are perishing'; real confidence mixed with panic (Mark's 'carest thou not?'); where the endangered should always go.
They leaned too much on his bodily presence and expostulated, instead of trusting his Divinity, unoppressed by sleep.
Gentle reproof ('of little faith'); rebuking the storm as a hostile power (cf. fever, frenzy); the word awes the tempest — proof of deity.
Mark's 'Peace, be still' shows the power of his voice reaching senseless elements, as God makes wars to cease (Ps 46:9); spirits composed first.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the sign of Jonah and the resurrection, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: A nature miracle proving deity (against the 'coincidence' evasion); 'men' suggests others besides the disciples; marvel joined to great fear.
Probably spoken by others not yet knowing Christ; the miracle proved his glory; 'the sleeping showed the man, the calm the God' (Gloss/Chrysostom).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the sign of Jonah, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Gadara/Gerasa/Gergesa one region (Khersa by the lake); Matthew's two demoniacs vs the one notable man of Mark/Luke; tombs the haunt of madmen.
The two demoniacs type the Gentile world (nations of Ham and Japheth) held among the tombs outside the synagogue, now visited by Christ.
The demons come to proclaim his divinity, that those who missed the calmed sea might hear them cry.

sides.position: two, Mark and Luke naming only the more notable one

schools.summary: 'What have we to do with thee' a Hebrew idiom of refusal; 'Son of the Most High God' from exorcism formulae; the demons dread 'torment before the time.'
A confession extorted by necessity, from suspicion not full knowledge (1 Cor 2:8); their witness condemns Jews and Arians (Remigius).
The devils breathe rebellion yet are crushed in a moment, compelled to yield though never subdued.

debates.question: Did the demons truly know Jesus to be the Son of God?

sides.position: they suspected rather than knew (else they had not crucified him)
the devil knew Jesus clearly; his agents recognized him

outliers.note: Stray fragment listing the witnesses to Christ, unrelated to the verse.

themes: what have we to do with thee

schools.summary: A herd of 2000 (Mk 5:13) 'a good way off' yet in sight; Jews forbidden to keep swine, so kept unlawfully or to supply Roman soldiers (Ellicott).
If Jewish property, kept in violation of the law, so their loss was just.

debates.question: Why were swine kept in Israel?

sides.position: kept to supply the Roman garrison

schools.summary: They beg not to be sent out of the country or into the abyss/deep (Rev 9; Lk's 'deep'); 'send us' shows Christ's absolute power — they cannot move without his word.
Their hidden aim: to provoke the owners to curse God over the loss, as Satan struck Job's house; demons do nothing without divine permission.
'Go' = permission; they rushed down the cliff into the sea; the rationalist 'frenzy drove them' rejected, since Christ's word sanctioned it; Christ's power over demons.
God hears demons only when their prayer echoes his justice; the loss a just punishment and a proof of the reality of the possession.
The keepers (of an infamous trade) fled in fear and amazement, carrying word of the swine and the healed demoniacs into the city.
Allegory: even the leaders of the wicked, shunning Christianity, proclaim the wonderful power of Christ.
They came perhaps to arrest him but, awed, only begged him to leave; the power of avarice, preferring swine to the Saviour and to souls.
'They loved their swine better than their souls! How many are of the same mind!'

themes: healed man clothed and in his right mind
